abstract: On-policy distillation (OPD) has become a popular training paradigm in the LLM community. This paradigm selects a larger model as the teacher to provide dense, fine-grained signals for each sampled trajectory, in contrast to reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), which only obtains sparse signals from verifiable outcomes in the environment. Recently, the community has explored on-policy self-distillation (OPSD), where the same model serves as both teacher and student, with the teacher receiving additional privileged information such as reference answers to enable self-evolution. This paper demonstrates that learning signals solely derived from the privileged teacher result in severe information leakage and unstable long-term training. Accordingly, we identify the optimal niche for self-distillation and propose \textbf{RLSD} (\textbf{RL}VR with \textbf{S}elf-\textbf{D}istillation). Specifically, we leverage self-distillation to obtain token-level policy differences for determining fine-grained update magnitudes, while continuing to use RLVR to derive reliable update directions from environmental feedback (e.g., response correctness). This enables RLSD to simultaneously harness the strengths of both RLVR and OPSD, achieving a higher convergence ceiling and superior training stability.

citations.fullCitation: [1] Zhihong Shao, Peiyi Wang, Qihao Zhu, Runxin Xu, Junxiao Song, Xiao Bi, Haowei Zhang, Mingchuan Zhang, Y. K. Li, Y. Wu, and Daya Guo. Deepseekmath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models, 2024. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2402.03300.
citations.justification: This paper introduces Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), which is the foundational Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) method that the main paper builds upon and compares against. The proposed RLSD method is presented as a direct enhancement to GRPO's advantage estimation, making this citation central to understanding the baseline and contribution.
citations.title: Deepseekmath: Pushing the limits of mathematical reasoning in open language models

citations.fullCitation: [7] Siyan Zhao, Zhihui Xie, Mengchen Liu, Jing Huang, Guan Pang, Feiyu Chen, and Aditya Grover. Self-distilled reasoner: On-policy self-distillation for large language models, 2026. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.18734.
citations.justification: This paper introduces a key On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) method. The main paper's core motivation is to diagnose the structural failures of the OPSD paradigm, such as information leakage and performance degradation, making this work fundamental to the problem being solved.
citations.title: Self-distilled reasoner: On-policy self-distillation for large language models

citations.fullCitation: [4] Rishabh Agarwal, Nino Vieillard, Yongchao Zhou, Piotr Stanczyk, Sabela Ramos Garea, Matthieu Geist, and Olivier Bachem. On-policy distillation of language models: Learning from self-generated mistakes. In The Twelfth International Conference on Learning Representations, 2024. URL https://openreview.net/forum?id=3zKtaqxLhW.
citations.justification: This paper introduces On-Policy Distillation (OPD), which serves as a crucial point of comparison to On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD). The authors frame their core research question around why OPD succeeds while OPSD fails, making this work essential for the theoretical context of the paper.
citations.title: On-policy distillation of language models: Learning from self-generated mistakes

citations.fullCitation: [8] Jonas Hübotter, Frederike Lübeck, Lejs Behric, Anton Baumann, Marco Bagatella, Daniel Marta, Ido Hakimi, Idan Shenfeld, Thomas Kleine Buening, Carlos Guestrin, and Andreas Krause. Reinforcement learning via self-distillation, 2026. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.20802.
citations.justification: This work, along with reference [7], defines the On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) paradigm that is critically analyzed by the authors. The paper's experiments explicitly include this method (as SDPO) as a key baseline for comparison, demonstrating its direct relevance.
citations.title: Reinforcement learning via self-distillation

citations.fullCitation: [9] John Schulman, Filip Wolski, Prafulla Dhariwal, Alec Radford, and Oleg Klimov. Proximal policy optimization algorithms, 2017. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/1707.06347.
citations.justification: This paper introduces Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), a foundational algorithm in modern reinforcement learning. Its clipped surrogate objective is explicitly mentioned as the design philosophy behind both the GRPO baseline and the proposed RLSD method, making it a key conceptual precursor to the paper's methodology.
citations.title: Proximal policy optimization algorithms

intermediateReport:
## Research Paper Analysis: Self-Distilled RLVR

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The authors of this research paper are Chenxu Yang, Chuanyu Qin, Minghui Chen, Naibin Gu, Dingyu Yao, Zheng Lin, and Weiping Wang, affiliated with the Institute of Information Engineering, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China, and the School of Cyber Security, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China. Additionally, Qingyi Si, Jiaqi Wang, and Nan Duan are affiliated with JD.COM. Chenxu Yang, Chuanyu Qin, and Qingyi Si are noted as equal contributors, and Zheng Lin is the corresponding author.

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

This work is situated within the field of post-training large language models (LLMs), particularly focusing on enhancing their reasoning capabilities. Two primary paradigms are prevalent: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) and On-Policy Distillation (OPD).

RLVR methods, such as Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), are widely used for training reasoning models. They rely on sparse, sequence-level scalar signals derived from environmental outcomes (e.g., correctness). A key limitation of RLVR is its coarse credit assignment; all tokens within a response receive the same advantage, regardless of their individual contribution to the final outcome. This makes fine-grained credit assignment for long-horizon reasoning a significant challenge. Prior work has attempted to address this through process reward models or step-level value estimators, often incurring additional computational overhead or requiring human annotations. Other approaches involve model-internal heuristics like entropy or attention dynamics for token-level importance estimation.

OPD addresses the sparse reward problem by employing a stronger, separate teacher model to provide dense, token-level supervision. This paradigm can accelerate convergence by enriching the learning signals. However, standard OPD requires maintaining a distinct and often larger teacher model, leading to substantial computational costs and the constraint of sharing the same vocabulary between teacher and student.

On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) emerged as an alternative to mitigate the overhead of external teachers. In OPSD, the same model acts as both teacher and student, with the teacher component conditioned on privileged information (e.g., a verified reasoning trace or environmental feedback). This approach aims to provide dense, per-token supervision without needing an external model. Despite its potential, the paper identifies and analyzes fundamental limitations of existing OPSD methods, namely information leakage and training instability.

The presented work, RLSD (RLVR with Self-Distillation), aims to synthesize the strengths of RLVR and OPSD. It seeks to combine the reliable, environment-anchored update directions of RLVR with the dense, token-level credit assignment offered by self-distillation, while mitigating the stability and leakage issues observed in previous OPSD approaches. This positions RLSD as an advancement that refines how privileged information is utilized in self-distillation to improve the efficiency and stability of training reasoning LLMs.

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objectives of this research are twofold: first, to identify and formally analyze the structural deficiencies of existing On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) methods; and second, to propose a new training paradigm, RLSD, that addresses these deficiencies while integrating the benefits of both RLVR and self-distillation.

The motivation for this work arises from several observations and challenges in the post-training of large language models for reasoning tasks:

1.  **Limitations of RLVR:** Traditional Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods, such as GRPO, receive only sparse, sequence-level feedback (e.g., a binary reward for correctness). This coarse signal makes it difficult to assign fine-grained credit or blame to individual tokens or reasoning steps within a generated response. Consequently, this can lead to slower convergence and suboptimal learning, as pivotal deductions receive the same update magnitude as incidental stylistic elements.

2.  **Promise of On-Policy Distillation (OPD):** OPD offers a solution to the sparse reward problem by using a more capable teacher model to provide dense, token-level supervision. This rich feedback can accelerate training and improve performance.

3.  **Challenges of OPD and Motivation for OPSD:** Standard OPD necessitates a separate, often larger, teacher model, which introduces significant computational overhead and scalability limitations (e.g., requirement for shared vocabulary). On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) was developed to overcome this by having the same model serve as both teacher and student, with the teacher conditionally aware of privileged information (e.g., a correct reasoning trace). This promises efficient, dense supervision without an external model.

4.  **Empirical Failure of Existing OPSD:** Despite its theoretical appeal, the paper observes that existing OPSD methods exhibit critical issues. Empirically, OPSD-trained models demonstrate a progressive degradation in performance after an initial peak, accompanied by systematic "privileged information leakage." This leakage manifests as the model explicitly referencing unseen "reference solutions" during inference, indicating that it has encoded information inaccessible during test time. Furthermore, the on-policy KL divergence between teacher and student under OPSD stagnates, failing to achieve sustained reduction, which suggests an underlying theoretical problem preventing convergence.

5.  **Identifying the Root Cause:** The central motivation is to understand *why* OPSD fails, particularly when OPD can be effective. The paper hypothesizes that the "information asymmetry" in OPSD—where the teacher has access to privileged information (`r`) that the student does not—creates a fundamental mismatch in the distribution-matching objective. This asymmetry, it is argued, makes the OPSD objective ill-posed.

The key objective for RLSD is therefore to leverage the dense, token-level signals from self-distillation, which are crucial for fine-grained credit assignment, while simultaneously preserving the reliability and grounding of environment rewards. This involves decoupling the roles of the privileged teacher: instead of serving as a generative target (as in OPSD), the teacher's information is repurposed to modulate the *magnitude* of updates, while the environment reward exclusively dictates the *direction* (reinforcement or penalization). This approach aims to achieve a higher convergence ceiling and superior training stability compared to existing paradigms.

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The methodology of this research first involves a theoretical and empirical analysis of On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) to understand its limitations, followed by the proposal and implementation of RLSD, a new training paradigm.

**Analysis of OPSD Failure:**
The paper conducts both empirical observations and a formal analysis to diagnose OPSD's shortcomings:
*   **Empirical Observations:** Experiments demonstrate that OPSD-trained models progressively exhibit "privileged information leakage," where they reference unavailable reference solutions during inference. This leakage correlates with a decline in validation accuracy after an early peak. Additionally, the KL divergence between teacher and student policies stagnates under OPSD, suggesting a failure to converge.
*   **Formal Analysis (Theoretical Diagnosis):**
    *   **Irreducible Mutual Information Gap (Theorem 1):** The core of the analysis proves that the OPSD objective, which forces a conditionally independent student policy `P_S(·|x)` to match a conditionally dependent teacher target `P_T(·|x,r)`, contains an irreducible mutual information gap `I(Y_t; R | X, Y_<t>)`. This gap is independent of the student's parameters and prevents true convergence, creating a lower bound on the loss that cannot be eliminated.
    *   **Gradient Structure and Leakage Mechanism (Proposition 1):** The analysis further shows that while the *expected* OPSD gradient is benign, per-sample gradients are corrupted by an `r`-specific deviation term. The variance of this deviation is proportional to the mutual information gap. This deviation, although having zero mean, causes parameter updates to encode statistical correlations between the input `x` and the privileged information `r`, leading to leakage.
    *   **Two-Phase Training Dynamics:** This theoretical understanding explains the observed empirical pattern: initial gains as the beneficial gradient component dominates, followed by performance degradation as the deviation component takes over and drives the model toward spurious `x → r` correlations.
    *   **Impossibility Trilemma (Appendix A.4):** Under shared parameters between teacher and student, the paper demonstrates that objective stability, sustained improvement, and leakage-free training cannot be simultaneously achieved in distribution-matching OPSD.

**RLSD (RLVR with Self-Distillation) Approach:**
Based on the diagnosis that distribution matching's failure stems from privileged information entering the gradient *direction*, RLSD proposes to repurpose the teacher's role from a generative target to a magnitude evaluator. The environment reward anchors the update direction, while the self-distilled signal modulates the update magnitude. The approach consists of three main steps integrated into the GRPO framework:

1.  **Privileged Information Gain (Δ_t):** For each token `y_t` in a student-sampled trajectory, the method computes the difference between its log-probability under the teacher context (`x` and privileged information `r`) and the student context (`x` only). This is defined as `Δ_t = sg(log P_T(y_t | x, r, y_<t>) - log P_S(y_t | x, y_<t>))`. The `sg` (stop-gradient) operator ensures that `Δ_t` serves purely as a weighting signal without creating auxiliary gradient pathways. This `Δ_t` quantifies how much the privileged information `r` supports or disfavors the generation of `y_t`.

2.  **Direction-Aware Evidence Reweighting (w_t):** A per-token weight `w_t` is constructed by exponentiating `Δ_t`, modulated by the sign of the sequence-level advantage `A` obtained from the environment (as in GRPO): `w_t = exp(sign(A) * Δ_t)`.
    *   If `A > 0` (correct trajectory), `w_t = P_T(y_t)/P_S(y_t)`. Tokens strongly supported by `r` (where `P_T` is much higher than `P_S`) receive larger weights, concentrating positive credit.
    *   If `A < 0` (incorrect trajectory), `w_t = P_S(y_t)/P_T(y_t)`. Tokens strongly disfavored by `r` (where `P_S` is much higher than `P_T`) receive larger weights, concentrating negative credit (blame).
    *   This formulation ensures `w_t > 0`, thus preserving the sign of `A`. The environment reward maintains exclusive control over the update direction (reinforce or penalize). An Appendix (A.5) provides a Bayesian interpretation, showing `w_t` as a sequential Bayesian belief update ratio for `r`.

3.  **Clipped Credit Assignment (Â_t):** To stabilize training, the evidence weights `w_t` are clipped within a range `[1-ϵ_w, 1+ϵ_w]`. The final token-level advantage `Â_t` is then calculated as `A * clip(w_t, 1-ϵ_w, 1+ϵ_w)`. For practical implementation, a mixing coefficient `λ` linearly interpolates between uniform advantage and the reweighted advantage over initial training steps, gradually shifting towards uniform advantage.

**Integration with GRPO:** The calculated `Â_t` serves as a direct replacement for the uniform advantage in the standard GRPO objective (Eq. 16), which remains a clipped surrogate objective. This means RLSD is a drop-in enhancement to existing RLVR pipelines without introducing auxiliary losses or models.

**Computational Cost:** The primary additional computational cost is one extra forward pass per response to obtain the teacher logits, which is noted as negligible compared to rollout generation.

**Privileged Information:** RLSD requires only the final ground-truth answer for `r`, making it less demanding than methods requiring full reasoning traces.

**Formal Guarantees (Appendix A.6):** The paper provides formal proofs that RLSD is structurally immune to privileged information leakage due to directional isolation (r cannot influence gradient sign), support isolation (gradients only act on student-sampled tokens), and magnitude boundedness (clipped scalar weights). It is also demonstrated that RLSD resolves the impossibility trilemma by simultaneously satisfying objective stability, sustained improvement, and leakage-free training.

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The research presents several main findings derived from both theoretical analysis and experimental validation:

**1. Identification of OPSD’s Structural Limitations:**
*   **Empirical Degradation:** OPSD methods exhibit an initial phase of performance improvement, followed by a decline in validation accuracy (Figure 1a, 3b). This degradation is accompanied by a progressive increase in "privileged information leakage," where the model explicitly references unseen reference solutions during inference (Figure 2, 3a).
*   **KL Divergence Stagnation:** The on-policy KL divergence between the OPSD teacher and student distributions rapidly drops but then plateaus, indicating a failure to achieve sustained convergence (Figure 3c).
*   **Theoretical Proof of Ill-Posed Objective:** Theorem 1 formally proves that OPSD's distribution-matching objective contains an irreducible mutual information gap `I(Y_t; R | X, Y_<t>)`. This gap is inherent to the information asymmetry between the privileged teacher and less-informed student and cannot be eliminated by optimization.
*   **Gradient-Level Leakage Mechanism:** Proposition 1 demonstrates that, while the *expected* OPSD gradient is benign, per-sample gradients include an `r`-specific deviation term. The variance of this deviation increases with the mutual information gap, driving the model to encode spurious correlations between input `x` and privileged information `r` into its parameters. This mechanism explains the observed information leakage and subsequent performance deterioration.
*   **Impossibility Trilemma:** The analysis in Appendix A.4 establishes that under shared parameters, OPSD (regardless of teacher freezing strategy) cannot simultaneously achieve objective stability, sustained improvement, and leakage-free training.

**2. Superior Performance of RLSD:**
*   **Highest Average Accuracy:** RLSD achieves the highest average accuracy across five challenging multimodal reasoning benchmarks (MMMU, MathVista, MathVision, ZeroBench, WeMath) (Table 2). Specifically, RLSD surpasses the Base LLM by 4.69% and standard GRPO by 2.32% on average for a 4K context length.
*   **Significant Gains on Reasoning Tasks:** RLSD demonstrates notable gains on mathematical reasoning datasets like MathVista (+1.9%) and MathVision (+3.91%) compared to GRPO, suggesting that its fine-grained credit assignment is particularly beneficial for complex, multi-step reasoning.
*   **Outperformance over Self-Distillation Baselines:** RLSD consistently outperforms other self-distillation approaches like OPSD and SDPO in average accuracy, validating the theoretical diagnosis that repurposing the teacher signal is more robust than direct distribution matching.

**3. Enhanced Training Dynamics:**
*   **Higher Convergence Ceiling and Stability:** RLSD exhibits a steeper initial ascent in reward and converges to a higher accuracy ceiling than GRPO, while notably avoiding the performance collapse observed in OPSD (Figure 1a, 5a). This demonstrates improved training stability.
*   **Maintained Entropy:** Unlike GRPO, which suffers from rapid entropy collapse due to uniform reward assignment, RLSD maintains a consistently higher entropy level throughout training (Figure 5b). This indicates that RLSD selectively strengthens critical reasoning tokens without uniformly suppressing alternatives, promoting more robust exploration and reasoning.
*   **Active Clipping Mechanism:** The clipping mechanism for credit assignment is actively engaged, with clip ratios stabilizing around 3-6% (Figure 5c), demonstrating its role as a trust-region constraint that bounds the teacher's per-token influence.

**4. Effective Fine-Grained Credit Assignment:**
*   **Qualitative Demonstration:** Case studies illustrate RLSD's ability to redistribute sequence-level reward effectively to the token level (Figure 6). In correct trajectories, RLSD assigns larger credit to tokens corresponding to decisive reasoning steps (e.g., counting, subtraction) while down-weighting generic narration. In incorrect trajectories, it concentrates blame on tokens representing misread information or erroneous calculations. This targeted credit assignment aligns with the design goal of modulating update *magnitudes* based on token contribution to correctness.

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The research makes significant contributions to the field of large language model training, particularly in the domain of reasoning, with several potential impacts:

1.  **Fundamental Understanding of Self-Distillation:** The paper provides a rigorous theoretical analysis identifying the root cause of performance degradation and information leakage in conventional on-policy self-distillation (OPSD) methods. This understanding, framed by the irreducible mutual information gap and gradient-level deviation, offers a foundational insight into the limitations of distribution-matching objectives when teacher and student operate under information asymmetry with shared parameters. This contribution can guide future research in designing more robust self-distillation techniques.

2.  **Advancement in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR):** RLSD addresses a core limitation of traditional RLVR methods like GRPO: the sparse, sequence-level nature of rewards. By effectively repurposing self-distillation to provide dense, token-level credit assignment *magnitudes* while retaining environment-anchored update *directions*, RLSD significantly enhances the granularity and efficiency of learning. This offers a principled alternative to methods relying on auxiliary reward models or heuristic proxies for token importance, which often incur higher computational costs or estimation noise.

3.  **Improved Model Performance and Training Stability:** The empirical results demonstrate that RLSD achieves higher average accuracy across diverse multimodal reasoning benchmarks compared to baseline LLMs and previous RLVR/self-distillation methods. Crucially, it combines the faster convergence observed with dense signals with the long-term training stability characteristic of RLVR, avoiding the performance collapse seen in existing OPSD. This suggests more robust and effective post-training for complex reasoning tasks.

4.  **Practical and Scalable Training Paradigm:** RLSD is designed as a drop-in replacement for the uniform advantage in standard GRPO, requiring only one additional forward pass per response for self-distillation. It also requires less demanding privileged information (only the final ground-truth answer) compared to methods needing full reasoning traces. This low computational overhead and minimal data requirement make RLSD a practical and scalable solution for improving LLMs.

5.  **Resolution of the Impossibility Trilemma:** By demonstrating that RLSD simultaneously satisfies objective stability, sustained improvement, and leakage-free training—properties that are mutually exclusive in distribution-matching OPSD—the research offers a robust framework for leveraging internal model knowledge without pathological side effects. This provides a blueprint for how to safely integrate privileged information into model optimization.

6.  **Broader Applicability:** While validated on multimodal reasoning tasks, the principles behind RLSD's design (decoupling update direction from magnitude, using internal discrepancies for fine-grained credit) could potentially be applied to other domains of LLM training or even broader reinforcement learning settings where sparse rewards and the need for internal credit assignment are prevalent. The framework of leveraging a privileged teacher signal for magnitude modulation, rather than generative imitation, may find utility in tasks requiring nuanced, token-level optimization.

In conclusion, this work offers both a critical theoretical diagnosis of a common self-distillation paradigm and a practical, robust solution that enhances the capabilities and stability of post-training large reasoning models, contributing to the development of more efficient and reliable AI systems.
overview:
The post-training of Large Language Models (LLMs) has shifted toward enhancing complex reasoning capabilities through two primary strategies: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) and On-Policy Distillation (OPD). While RLVR methods like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) use external signals (e.g., a binary indicator of mathematical correctness) to guide the model, they suffer from "coarse credit assignment." Every token in a long reasoning chain receives the same update signal, regardless of whether it was a brilliant logical deduction or a minor calculation error.

On the other hand, On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) attempts to solve this by using the model itself as a teacher. The teacher is given "privileged information"—such as the correct final answer—to generate dense, token-level supervision for the student (the same model without the answer). However, this work identifies a critical failure in current self-distillation: it often leads to "information leakage," where the model learns to hallucinate reference solutions during inference, eventually causing performance to collapse.

![Average performance dynamics and reasoning task comparison](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03128v1/x1.png)
*Figure 1: Performance dynamics on validation sets show that while OPSD (red) peaks early and then degrades, RLSD (green) achieves a higher convergence ceiling and maintains stability compared to standard GRPO (blue).*

To address these issues, the researchers propose **RLSD (RLVR with Self-Distillation)**. RLSD redefines the role of self-distillation: instead of the teacher serving as a target for the student to imitate, the teacher’s privileged knowledge is used only to modulate the *magnitude* of the update, while the environment reward (correctness) strictly controls the *direction*.

## The Structural Failure of Existing Self-Distillation

The paper begins by diagnosing why current OPSD methods fail. In a typical OPSD setup, a teacher policy $P_T(y_t | x, r, y_{<t})$ (conditioned on input $x$ and privileged information $r$) is used as a target for a student policy $P_S(y_t | x, y_{<t})$. The goal is to minimize the distribution difference between them.

Through empirical testing, the authors found that models trained with OPSD eventually start "leaking" information. For example, during testing (where no reference answer $r$ is provided), the model might suddenly output phrases like "The reference solution is..." or include internal indices that were only present in the training metadata. This indicates that the model has encoded statistical correlations between the input $x$ and the privileged answer $r$ directly into its weights.

![Leakage occurrence dynamics during training](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03128v1/x2.png)
*Figure 2: The count of "leakage" events—where the model references privileged information—increases significantly over time in OPSD, correlating with the eventual performance drop.*

The authors provide a formal theoretical diagnosis for this failure. They prove that because of the information asymmetry (the teacher knows $r$ but the student does not), there exists an irreducible mutual information gap $I(Y_t; R | X, Y_{<t})$. This gap acts as a lower bound on the loss function that the student can never reach. Because the student is forced to match a target it cannot fully see, the resulting gradients contain a "noise" term. While this noise averages out to zero across a full dataset, the per-sample updates drive the model to find spurious ways to "guess" $r$ from $x$, leading to the observed leakage and training instability.

![KL Divergence and validation performance](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03128v1/x3.png)
*Figure 3: Under OPSD, the KL divergence between teacher and student plateaus early, and performance declines after an initial peak, unlike stable distillation methods.*

## Bridging RLVR and Self-Distillation with RLSD

RLSD is designed to capture the dense, token-level insights of distillation without the risks of distribution matching. The core innovation is **Directional Isolation**. 

In RLSD, the environment provides a sequence-level advantage $A$. This $A$ is positive if the model's answer was correct and negative if it was wrong. This $A$ is the *only* component that decides whether to reinforce or penalize a sequence of tokens. The self-distillation signal—the difference between what the teacher knows and what the student knows—is used solely to adjust how much we should reinforce or penalize *each specific token*.

![RLSD Framework Overview](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03128v1/x4.png)
*Figure 4: The RLSD framework. The environment reward determines the update direction (sign), while the self-distillation signal (the difference between Teacher and Student modes) modulates the magnitude of the update for each token.*

This approach avoids the "Impossibility Trilemma" identified by the authors: that a model cannot simultaneously achieve objective stability, sustained improvement, and leakage-free training when the teacher is used as a generative target. By using the teacher only as a "magnitude evaluator," RLSD keeps the gradient direction anchored to the verifiable ground truth.

## The Mechanics of RLSD

The implementation of RLSD follows a three-step process integrated into the standard RL training loop:

### 1. Privileged Information Gain ($\Delta_t$)
For every token $y_t$ generated by the student, RLSD calculates the "gain" provided by the privileged information $r$. This is defined as:
$$\Delta_t = \text{sg}(\log P_T(y_t | x, r, y_{<t}) - \log P_S(y_t | x, y_{<t}))$$
The `sg` operator (stop-gradient) is crucial; it ensures that we are only using this value as a scalar weight and not backpropagating through the teacher's calculation. This $\Delta_t$ essentially asks: "Knowing the correct answer, how much more (or less) likely is this specific token?"

### 2. Direction-Aware Weighting ($w_t$)
The gain is then converted into a weight $w_t$ that respects the outcome of the entire reasoning chain. The weight is calculated as:
$$w_t = \exp(\text{sign}(A) \cdot \Delta_t) = \left( \frac{P_T}{P_S} \right)^{\text{sign}(A)}$$
- If the trajectory was **correct** ($A > 0$), then $w_t = P_T/P_S$. Tokens that the teacher strongly supports get a larger weight, receiving more "credit" for the correct answer.
- If the trajectory was **incorrect** ($A < 0$), then $w_t = P_S/P_T$. Tokens that the teacher strongly disfavors (where the student was much more confident than the teacher who knew the answer) get a larger weight, receiving more "blame" for the failure.

Because $w_t$ is always positive, the sign of the original advantage $A$ is never flipped. The environment reward remains the "source of truth" for the direction of learning.

### 3. Clipped Credit Assignment ($\hat{A}_t$)
To prevent any single token from dominating the training update, the weights are clipped within a range $[1-\epsilon_w, 1+\epsilon_w]$. The final token-level advantage $\hat{A}_t$ is:
$$\hat{A}_t = A \cdot \text{clip}(w_t, 1-\epsilon_w, 1+\epsilon_w)$$
This creates a "trust region" where the internal self-distillation signal can refine the reward, but the external environment reward always maintains control.

## Experimental Evaluation and Findings

The researchers evaluated RLSD on five major multimodal reasoning benchmarks: MMMU, MathVista, MathVision, ZeroBench, and WeMath. Using a Qwen2-VL-8B-Instruct model as the base, they compared RLSD against standard GRPO and various self-distillation baselines.

**Superior Performance:** RLSD consistently achieved the highest average accuracy. On average, it outperformed the base model by 4.69% and standard GRPO by 2.32%. The gains were particularly significant on math-heavy tasks like MathVision (+3.91% over GRPO), suggesting that fine-grained credit assignment is highly effective for complex multi-step deductions.

**Training Stability:** Unlike OPSD, which collapses after a certain number of steps, RLSD remains stable throughout training. It achieves a higher convergence ceiling than GRPO, proving that the dense token-level signals help the model learn more efficiently.

![Training dynamics: reward, entropy, and clipping](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.03128v1/x5.png)
*Figure 5: RLSD maintains higher training entropy compared to GRPO, which often suffers from "entropy collapse" where it prematurely converges to a few common patterns. The clipping ratios show that the model actively uses the self-distilled signal throughout training.*

**Qualitative Impact:** Through case studies, the authors demonstrated that RLSD effectively identifies "pivotal" tokens. In a correct reasoning trace, higher credit was assigned to the exact moments of logical transition (e.g., performing a subtraction or counting items in an image). In incorrect traces, the model was able to pinpoint specific calculation errors and apply more "blame" to those tokens than to the rest of the response.

## Significance of the Work

The primary contribution of this research is the theoretical and practical decoupling of the teacher's role in self-distillation. By identifying the inherent "mutual information gap" that makes distribution-matching OPSD ill-posed, the authors provide a clear explanation for why many reasoning models fail during long-duration post-training.

RLSD provides a robust, scalable solution that:
1.  **Eliminates Information Leakage:** Because the teacher cannot influence the sign of the gradient, the model is prevented from learning spurious correlations to the privileged answer.
2.  **Solves the Credit Assignment Problem:** It provides a principled way to turn sequence-level rewards into token-level insights without needing expensive human-annotated process rewards or external teacher models.
3.  **Maintains Exploration:** By avoiding uniform reward assignment, RLSD prevents the rapid entropy collapse seen in standard RLVR, allowing the model to continue exploring diverse reasoning paths.

As a drop-in replacement for existing RLVR pipelines, RLSD offers a more efficient path toward building models that not only get the right answer but understand which parts of their internal logic were responsible for that success.

keyInsights: Existing OPSD methods fail due to an inherent 'irreducible mutual information gap' between the privileged teacher and less-informed student, which prevents true convergence and leads to information leakage via corrupted gradients.

Decoupling the roles of privileged information—using it to modulate update *magnitudes* rather than as a generative target for distribution matching—resolves OPSD's fundamental issues of leakage and instability.

Fine-grained, token-level credit assignment, when controlled by the environment reward's direction and scaled by self-distilled magnitude, significantly improves the efficiency, stability, and convergence ceiling of reasoning LLM training.
originalProblem: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods struggle with sparse, sequence-level rewards, leading to coarse credit assignment for long-horizon reasoning tasks.
On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) promises dense, token-level supervision but empirically exhibits information leakage and training instability, causing performance degradation.
Standard On-Policy Distillation (OPD) requires a separate, often larger, teacher model, leading to substantial computational overhead and scalability limitations.
results: RLSD achieved the highest average accuracy across five multimodal reasoning benchmarks, surpassing the Base LLM by 4.69% and standard GRPO by 2.32% on average for a 4K context length.
RLSD demonstrated superior training stability and a higher convergence ceiling compared to GRPO, effectively avoiding the performance degradation and information leakage observed in OPSD.

The method effectively assigns fine-grained credit, distributing larger credit to decisive reasoning steps in correct trajectories and concentrating blame on erroneous calculations in incorrect ones, while maintaining higher entropy throughout training.
solution: A theoretical and empirical analysis formally proves that OPSD's distribution-matching objective contains an irreducible mutual information gap, causing gradient corruption and information leakage.

RLSD (RLVR with Self-Distillation) is proposed to repurpose the privileged teacher signal, using it solely to modulate the *magnitude* of token-level credit assignment, while environment rewards dictate the update *direction*.

RLSD integrates with GRPO by computing a direction-aware evidence reweighting factor (`P_T/P_S` or `P_S/P_T`) for each token, which is then clipped and used to replace the uniform advantage in the GRPO objective.
summary.summary: Researchers at the Chinese Academy of Sciences and JD.COM formally analyzed fundamental limitations in existing On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD) for training reasoning LLMs, then proposed a new paradigm, RLSD, that separates environment-anchored update direction from self-distilled update magnitude. RLSD achieves an average of 2.32% absolute accuracy improvement over standard GRPO on multimodal reasoning benchmarks while ensuring training stability and preventing information leakage.

title: Self-Distilled RLVR
